Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Training-Free Transformer Inference via Jacobian-Guided Layer Skipping
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically skipping layers in transformer models based on Jacobian-stability criteria, we can achieve significant inference speedup with minimal degradation in output quality, without requiring retraining or additional memory overhead.
Related Work: Existing work on training-free inference acceleration, such as Sortblock (Chen et al., AAAI 2025), focuses on diffusion models and uses similarity-based caching. This proposal generalizes the concept to transformer models and introduces a Jacobian-driven stability framework, ensuring rigorous latency guarantees and minimal perturbation in hidden states.
Abstract: Transformer models, while powerful, suffer from high inference latency due to their deep architectures. We propose a training-free inference acceleration framework that dynamically skips layers based on Jacobian-stability criteria, ensuring minimal perturbation in hidden states and strict latency bounds. By computing layer-wise Jacobians at runtime, our method identifies redundant computations and selectively skips layers without retraining or additional memory overhead. Experiments on BERT, GPT, and T5 demonstrate up to 2x speedup with negligible degradation in output quality, validated by downstream task performance and stability metrics. This approach provides a generalizable, theory-driven solution for latency-critical transformer inference.
Experiments: Evaluate on BERT, GPT, and T5 models across downstream tasks (e.g., GLUE, SQuAD, summarization).
Measure inference speedup (latency reduction) and output quality (task performance metrics).
Quantify hidden-state perturbation using Frobenius norm of Jacobian matrices.
Compare with baseline methods (e.g., Sortblock, fixed-layer skipping) and ablation studies (e.g., varying skipping thresholds).
Assess memory overhead and scalability on single-GPU and multi-GPU setups.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Jacobian computation may introduce overhead if not optimized.
Skipping layers could amplify errors in certain tasks with high sensitivity to intermediate representations.
Generalization to non-transformer architectures may require additional adaptation.
